words: KAREN PURTEE: I thought it would be nice if we started back where you said you
were living before you came here and why your father decided to come
here, and then about your train ride and all that was part of that.
HELENA CARTWRIGHT CARLSON:  You mean clear back to Minnesota?

speaker: KP
words: Sure, why not?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Well, of course, I can't remember much about that. But I was born in

speaker: KP
words: Minnesota and my parents moved- oh, in fact, I guess my father lived

speaker: KP
words: in South Dakota, and he was courting my mother in Minnesota and he

speaker: KP
words: came over there. And^when they got married they moved to- I don't

speaker: KP
words: know how come I was born in Minnesota, if they were living in South

speaker: KP
words: Dakota; that's a good question, you see I can't remember because I

speaker: KP
words: was too young. Apparently.

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Anyway, we were in South Dakota. About the first instance of anything

speaker: KP
words: that I can remember and that was the time I swallowed a barley straw.

speaker: KP
words: We were on the way to church and it was in the winter in a bobsled

speaker: KP
words: and what I had a barley straw in my mouth for, but anyway, it got a

speaker: KP
words: hold and it commenced to crawl down and crawled and crawled and mother

speaker: KP
words: said, "Don't swallow, don't swallow." And. got the team turned around

speaker: KP
words: and headed for home. And you know what I did, I just swallowed and

speaker: KP
words: swallowed just as hard and fast as I could and finally I said, "It's

speaker: KP
words: , it's gone!" But it was too late^so we just kept on going. And

speaker: KP
words: then I remember of getting a pair of shoes. It must have been there

speaker: KP
words: and I was so tiny I can't remember, but, except they said, "How do

speaker: KP
words: they feel?" And I was a little bit of a kid sitting there, "Oh, fine."

speaker: KP
words: And father missed me went outside and when he came back I'd gone back inside because

speaker: KP
words: I

speaker: KP
words: wanted another pair of shoes.

speaker: KP
words: Then we came out West and settled in an orchard tract south of

speaker: KP
words: Spokane. That orchard business wasn't suitable to my dad, he didn't

speaker: KP
words: want to raise fruit, so they bought a place up north of Spokane at

speaker: KP
words: Valley, Washington, where he wanted to raise cows; dairy. He was in

speaker: KP
words: terested in dairying. So they had a large herd of dairy cattle and

speaker: KP
words: amongst them were some wild ones, wild cows and the only one that

speaker: KP
words: could handle them was my dad. And so when he'd have to be gone he'd

speaker: KP
words: have to get someone else and they had 'em all- oh, timbers to hold

speaker: KP
words: them so that when anyone would milk them they'd have to sit on the

speaker: KP
words: outside and reach underneath these timbers cause they kicked like

speaker: KP
words: mules. Fought like fiends, you just can't imagine that was as wild

speaker: KP
words: as those cows were. But they weren't all wild. But anyway, we were

speaker: KP
words: there I think it was four years. And that's where I started to school.

speaker: KP
words: Well, when I was south of Spokane I had got old enough, I guess, but

speaker: KP
words: my mother had taught me so I could read and I'd learned a few lessons

speaker: KP
words: in obedience .there, because that's when I had the little doll buggy

speaker: KP
words: and my sister had a doll buggy and we were wheeling it around the field

speaker: KP
words: and we wanted to go over to the neighbors and she said we couldn't,

speaker: KP
words: so we wheeled around the fields and wheeled around the fields til we

speaker: KP
words: got real close, we got to the fence that was separating us from the

speaker: KP
words: neighbors and we decided as long as we had got that close we'd just

speaker: KP
words: slide under the fence and go in just long enough to say "Hello." and

speaker: KP
words: leave. Which we did. But when.we got back we got a good paddling.

speaker: KP
words: So we didn't try that anymore. (Chuckles) But anyhow- after we'd moved

speaker: KP
words: to Valley, I went to school there and it took me six weeks to finish

speaker: KP
words: the first grade because my mother had done a real good job of teaching

speaker: KP
words: me, so I ended the first grade. I suppose maybe we got clear to the

speaker: KP
words: end of the term or something, anyway, I was very shy and very fearful

speaker: KP
words: there. And we had a teacher- I suppose I would have been in the se

speaker: KP
words: cond or third grade and we had a little teacher, her name was Tillie

speaker: KP
words: Cole, by the way, and she was one of these, I think she'd made a re

speaker: KP
words: solution that before she finished that term of school she was going

speaker: KP
words: to give everybody in school a good licking! And I think she did.

speaker: KP
words: She missed me someway or other, but I got threatened if I ever did

speaker: KP
words: another stunt like I had done, which was just- we were practicing a

speaker: KP
words: drill for a program and I stopped too soon in the drill, I was sup

speaker: KP
words: posed to have gone clear around the room, instead I stopped at the

speaker: KP
words: end where I was supposed to wind up and she said, "if you ever do that

speaker: KP
words: again, you're going to get a whipping!"

speaker: KP
words: What kind of a drill?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Yeah, a drill, oh, I suppose there were about ten, twelve kids and

speaker: KP
words: they lined up and I was one of the little ones at one end, there was

speaker: KP
words: another girl, a little one at the other end, and they divided, see,

speaker: KP
words: and they'd go like this and they make fancy movements-

speaker: KP
words: Oh, it was a walking drill?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Yes, a walking drill. And I was at this one end and we were supposed

speaker: KP
words: to go and meet like this, see, some way or other and I was supposed

speaker: KP
words: to wind - go clear across and wind up at this end and when I got to

speaker: KP
words: that end, I stopped. And that spoiled the looks of the drill, I stop

speaker: KP
words: ped, yet I was supposed to have gone clear around and come back.

speaker: KP
words: Did you do that to music?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Yes, to music.

speaker: KP
words: Yes, to music, huh?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  They'd put on drills, that was one thing they used to do in school.

speaker: KP
words: That was one of their fancy things they did, was put on fancy drills.

speaker: KP
words: They'd dress 'em and fix 'em up real pretty and they must have been

speaker: KP
words: beautiful. I'm sure they were.

speaker: KP
words: For their parents to come and see?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Yes. And they'd have the program for the parents to come. And so,

speaker: KP
words: I just remember that one. And my little younger sister, I remember

speaker: KP
words: she got whopping! She was daydreaming, sitting looking out the

speaker: KP
words: window thinking of things outside, I guess, and she got threatened

speaker: KP
words: once, I believe she was threatened once, the next time she got it!

speaker: KP
words: (Chuckles) I felt worse than she did, I guess. Anyway then- My dad

speaker: KP
words: said if he could find a place- No, he decided that he'd sell out, he

speaker: KP
words: didn't like it there, so after we'd been there for four years, why,

speaker: KP
words: he moved to, oh, it must have been- moved to Spokane, in transition

speaker: KP
words: from one place to another til he found another place. So we lived in

speaker: KP
words: Hillyard and he worked in the machine shops. But he wasn't cut out

speaker: KP
words: for a machineshop man. They had more trouble that winter that we

speaker: KP
words: were there, I'm sure, than they'\k ever had. He was sick and got hurt.

speaker: KP
words: And everybody was sick and^terrible things happen to them. So, my

speaker: KP
words: dad then started out and he said if he could find a place where they

speaker: KP
words: could raise grain and hay, he was going to get it and go back into

speaker: KP
words: the dairying. And I don't remember how it happened, anyway how he

speaker: KP
words: happened to hear about Troy. Anyway he came to Troy and bought, I

speaker: KP
words: believe it's an eighty acre farm out here and he came back. And we

speaker: KP
words: were all so thoroughly delighted because we were going to move back on

speaker: KP
words: the farm. None of us liked :ity life. We did not like it! So

speaker: KP
words: we were happy as larks to get the chance to move back. And while we

speaker: KP
words: were in Hilljrard we had acquired a little kitten which we named Tommy

speaker: KP
words: and a little brown pullet, chicken had come there which turned into a

speaker: KP
words: pullet. was just a chicken when we got her; how we acquired her

speaker: KP
words: I don't know. Anyway, she had grown up to a nice pullet and we couldn't

speaker: KP
words: bear to leave and not take our kitten and our pullet with us. So that

speaker: KP
words: was our livestock! They fixed us each a box so my sister carried one

speaker: KP
words: and I carried the other and we boarded the train carrrying our live

speaker: KP
words: stock with us! And we came on the train to Troy and there were a lot

speaker: KP
words: of men on the train, and they were talking a language we never had

speaker: KP
words: heard. We couldn't understand what they were talking about and a lot

speaker: KP
words: of 'em got off at Troy. And afterward, we heard that they were lumber

speaker: KP
words: jacks, and they had been to Spokane for a weekend or something, I guess,

speaker: KP
words: which was the big highlight of the lumberjacks at those times. They'd

speaker: KP
words: work and earn some money then they'd go and have a gay time in

speaker: KP
words: the^city! And apparently these were returning from a gay time in the

speaker: KP
words: city. And they used to say that the trainmen couldn't understand them

speaker: KP
words: either, but they thumped their hip pocket and if they felt a snuss

speaker: KP
words: can or hit a snuss can they threw them off at Troy. See, they were

speaker: KP
words: Scandinavians. Well, anyway, we arrived April first 1912 in Troy. And

speaker: KP
words: we stayed all night inKGreer Hotel, which was located where Jamie Arnetts-

speaker: KP
words: on that corner. There was a hotel there and we stayed there all

speaker: KP
words: night. And Griff Torrance came down to get us the next day in a lum

speaker: KP
words: ber wagon, practically hub deep in mud. That was Main Street at that

speaker: KP
words: time. Mud everywhere. And he was an oddball of a guy with a funny

speaker: KP
words: voice, which we got quite a kick out of. He had a squeeky voice. But

speaker: KP
words: he took us out to the farm and there we were! And the house still

speaker: KP
words: looks just like it did at that time. It may be a different color, I'm

speaker: KP
words: not sure, because it's had two, three coats of paints in the years,

speaker: KP
words: but it's a big square house; two story house sitting on a knoll and

speaker: KP
words: there it still sits. Only at that time when we got there, the trees came clear down

speaker: KP
words: to the back of the house. Out of the eighty acres,

speaker: KP
words: I hate to say how many was in cultivation, but the flat wasn't covered

speaker: KP
words: with trees, the meadow, and that's about all that wasn't I think. The

speaker: KP
words: rest was trees.

speaker: KP
words: Did your father do the clearing then?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Yes. They cleared. They cut trees and cut brush and piled sticks

speaker: KP
words: and burned. And we had a real good time doing it and hated every

speaker: KP
words: minute of it, I suppose! Us kids. But since then, I've learned to

speaker: KP
words: like that kind of work. You know, I thought it was real nice, but

speaker: KP
words: all of us, we worked out there and cleared land. In 1931 the big fire

speaker: KP
words: went through out there and took the trees over across the creek and

speaker: KP
words: so that has been cleared since. Most of it's in cultivation now, ex

speaker: KP
words: cept one little section, I guess still has trees. So it doesn't look

speaker: KP
words: quite like it did. We left a grove up back of the house for our pic

speaker: KP
words: nics and things like that, which we liked very much to do. And then

speaker: KP
words: we raised carrots for the cows. My dad went into cows, of course,

speaker: KP
words: Jerseys. And we raised the cows- in fact, Mother used to say that

speaker: KP
words: those cows, she said would wade belly deep in water to get over to

speaker: KP
words: the pump where Dad would fill the tub for them.,(Chuckles) Anyway,

speaker: KP
words: they had a barn- they built the barn, James Pence, his name was, he

speaker: KP
words: lived up there, built a barn and it's still standing sturdily. They

speaker: KP
words: claimed he was one of the best carpenters around. 1917 I believe the

speaker: KP
words: barn was built. Wish they'd put a nice coat of red paint on it and

speaker: KP
words: make it loot pretty. It was painted but it needs a new coat again.

speaker: KP
words: Who owns the place now?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Melvin Marsh. Anyway, while we were there went to school and we were

speaker: KP
words: quite unhappy when we went to school because because we had gone to

speaker: KP
words: this city school in Spokane and we thought we were quite smart. And

speaker: KP
words: when we got down here they set us back a grade because we weren't

speaker: KP
words: up to where they were. And that was a blow to our ego, of course!

speaker: KP
words: I think I was a fifth grader or six grader, I thought I'd made the

speaker: KP
words: sixth grade up there and I got chucked back into the fifth. I think

speaker: KP
words: maybe the main reason was because they probably didn't have a sixth

speaker: KP
words: grade out there and they didn't need an extra grade. (Chuckles) That's

speaker: KP
words: the way they did in the rural schools. I know we had a young teacher

speaker: KP
words: when I was supposed to have been in the seventh grade and she didn't

speaker: KP
words: have any other seventh graders so I was put in the eighth grade and

speaker: KP
words: missed the seventh grade, and that was not the thing to do because

speaker: KP
words: the seventh grade lays the foundation for the eighth grade in those

speaker: KP
words: days, so I had to take the eighth grade two years, which was a blow

speaker: KP
words: also to my ego. Anyway, then we had about two miles to school, I

speaker: KP
words: guess. We walked to school. And we walked there across some— when

speaker: KP
words: the road was good and the weather was good enough, why, we walked and

speaker: KP
words: we did a lot of walking. But in the winter when it was real bad and

speaker: KP
words: they did have snow- thoSfirst winter we were there it was six

speaker: KP
words: feet on the level. And I remember how excited we were when that

speaker: KP
words: snow was on, we tunnelled- we just had more fun in that snow. We

speaker: KP
words: never had heard of such a thing before, like that much snow. And

speaker: KP
words: when it started to thaw in the spring, why it crusted over and made

speaker: KP
words: crust, so it made the best coasting you could ever imagine. But our

speaker: KP
words: first experience with it when it had crusted over- I remember yet how

speaker: KP
words: excited- we were almost in hysterics and we went out, Th e moon was

speaker: KP
words: shining so bright and we'd just gone out there and we found out we

speaker: KP
words: could walk on that snow it was so hard and crusty. And I can just

speaker: KP
words: remember my sister and I just practically in hysterics, we just screamed

speaker: KP
words: because we could walk on the snow. We came in yelling at our parents.

speaker: KP
words: "Oh, we can walk on the snow!" And here we were. You just can imagine

speaker: KP
words: those two kids just galloping around out there. Then we found

speaker: KP
words: out that it made wonderful coasting and I tried even to do some skiing

speaker: KP
words: but my sister and I didn't get too well along on our skiis because

speaker: KP
words: our skiis weren't weren't trained, one wanted to go east and the other

speaker: KP
words: one west and north or south. We spent most of our time falling down.

speaker: KP
words: But I know the folks used to go out and coast with us. We had nice

speaker: KP
words: coasting hills and they'd go coasting too and that was fun. But, any

speaker: KP
words: way, back to our school- or should I mention about the creek going

speaker: KP
words: through the meadow? We had a beautiful creek through there. It's still

speaker: KP
words: there when the water's high, there's still a creek there and it was

speaker: KP
words: lined with the trees and bushes and it was beautiful. And it had fish

speaker: KP
words: in it. We had a neighbor that had a boy our age and he just spent

speaker: KP
words: most of his time on that creek fishing. And that was one of the plea

speaker: KP
words: sant memories that we have of that creek. And we played in the water

speaker: KP
words: and we played the creek and just had bark boats and leaf boats and

speaker: KP
words: all kinds of boats that we could float down this creek and then fish

speaker: KP
words: besides.

speaker: KP
words: Does it still kfcye fish?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Naw. After while they commenced to take the trees and one thing and

speaker: KP
words: another and progress, I guess, the creek dried up in the summer and

speaker: KP
words: the fish were gone. So no more fishing and no more water in the sum

speaker: KP
words: mer. They went to the source and took it to the towns using the water.

speaker: KP
words: Sad day. But anyway when we went to school- as I said, we had to walk

speaker: KP
words: to school but when the weather was bad my dad would hook up the team

speaker: KP
words: to the bobsled, which is the forerunners of the long sled and

speaker: KP
words: we'd wrap up in blankets and cover our faces to keep the horse hair

speaker: KP
words: out and away we'd go for school. And he'd take us to school. But

speaker: KP
words: some of 'em instead of taking their kids to school, they used to

speaker: KP
words: send them on a horse and some of 'em would ride a horse to school and

speaker: KP
words: then when they'd get there they'd turn the horse loose and it would

speaker: KP
words: go home. So, my dad decided rather than to take us he'd let us try

speaker: KP
words: it because we had an old gray horse there and he knew that she'd come

speaker: KP
words: home. So he parked the two of us/on the horse and we had to go- our

speaker: KP
words: house was situated and we had to go this way and then across the mea

speaker: KP
words: dow and then follow the meadow on that side, there was no way of going

speaker: KP
words: across it, so to stay on the road we'd go like this. So he put us on

speaker: KP
words: the horse and we went down like this and we crossed the meadow and

speaker: KP
words: then we got about half way across the meadow on the other side of the

speaker: KP
words: meadow and I don't know why, that I can't remember, why, but I think

speaker: KP
words: I was the one that for some reason or other slid off the horse. Whe

speaker: KP
words: ther I did it to see if I could get back on to practice or why, I

speaker: KP
words: do not know, but of course i couldn't get back on. We couldn't find

speaker: KP
words: a place to pull the horse up so I walked to school. And my sister

speaker: KP
words: rode the horse. And I remember how angry my father was. He thought

speaker: KP
words: we were so dumb. We never got a chance to try it again. And we car

speaker: KP
words: ried our lunches. We didn't have hot lunches in those days. But I

speaker: KP
words: was talking to Elmer Vim over here in the resthome and he said, "Yeah,"

speaker: KP
words: he said, "we carried it in lard buckets. Ten pound lard buckets."

speaker: KP
words: I don't remember that I had a ten pound lard bucket, cause mine was

speaker: KP
words: about that high and I think it was a five pound lard bucket. We car

speaker: KP
words: ried our lunches. And the nearest to a hot lunch that we had was

speaker: KP
words: that it froze on the way to school and we had to set it by the old,

speaker: KP
words: big heater in there to thaw it out! So all the dinner buckets were

speaker: KP
words: sitting around the heater there to keep from freezing. If we'd a

speaker: KP
words: left them out in the anteroom where the coats were they'd have frozen

speaker: KP
words: anyway, I guess, it was that cold. So we had to park around the

speaker: KP
words: heater. Anyway, we seemed to thrive on it. ^reason why those

speaker: KP
words: were nice buckets, too, they made handy if the boys got fresh, why,

speaker: KP
words: we could bat. 'em on the head with those buckets. It didn't hurt anybody but they

speaker: KP
words: served as a warning that they must not get too close!

speaker: KP
words: And eventually we did fall heir- somebody gave us a real treat, they

speaker: KP
words: got these tobacco cans that were quite long and shallow and had a

speaker: KP
words: lid that lifted up on a hinge. So we fell heir to tobacco cans for

speaker: KP
words: lunchbuckets,we could arrange our lunch in there very neatly and

speaker: KP
words: pile it up instead of having it all poked in one bucket. So we

speaker: KP
words: thought that was a real treat to have tobacco can for a lunch box.

speaker: KP
words: When the weather was good and the roads were good, some of the kids

speaker: KP
words: rode bicycles to school. And I tried desperately to learn to ride

speaker: KP
words: a bicycle. I remember there was one boy that I kind of liked pret

speaker: KP
words: ty well had a girl's bicycle too, and he let me try it. But after

speaker: KP
words: putting my foot through the spokas and things like that and falling

speaker: KP
words: off a few times, gave it up as a bad job. Guess he was glad, too!

speaker: KP
words: I can't remember just how we- where we had water when we came out to

speaker: KP
words: the farm, but I remember one of the first things that they did was

speaker: KP
words: to drill a well. Then we had a well right by the house; had a pump

speaker: KP
words: in it. But they had drilled quite deep and then there was sand

speaker: KP
words: down in there or something and it caved in, so we never had a very

speaker: KP
words: deep well and never had water enough really for- house use

speaker: KP
words: and of course, the creek went through so we had water for- because

speaker: KP
words: we had a creek down across our place and another place, too, there.

speaker: KP
words: And it was a kind of a spring apparently, it stayed water in it all

speaker: KP
words: the time, for the cattle. And they were down in the flat where the

speaker: KP
words: creek went so they had water that way. Eventually, the folks decided

speaker: KP
words: that they would put a cistern. They had put an orchard up on the

speaker: KP
words: hill then where they had cleared land and raised fruit up there,

speaker: KP
words: raspberries, and had a real lovely garden. Everything just about

speaker: KP
words: that you could imagine and raised a garden. So they built or dug a

speaker: KP
words: cistern up there and dug a well down by the creek and piped it up to

speaker: KP
words: the cistern, so then we had piped water into the house and we went

speaker: KP
words: modern! Before that we had these little outhouses out behind; we

speaker: KP
words: weren't modern people then. We had to travel out in the weather!

speaker: KP
words: (Chuckles) But anyhow, we had- they put in a bathroom. We had a

speaker: KP
words: bathrub and we had indoor water, hot and cold. They put in a hot

speaker: KP
words: water tank so we had hot and cold water. And they had water enough

speaker: KP
words: so they could irrigate to help the gardens. And that was a big boost

speaker: KP
words: too. And we were happy with that.

speaker: KP
words: Did you help your mother around the house a lot?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Yes.

speaker: KP
words: Or did you mostly help your father?

speaker: KP
words: CARLSON:  Well, I did both. In fact, I remember that we took turns doing

speaker: KP
words: dishes and I had the breakfast dishes; my sister had the supper dishes

speaker: KP
words: and we usually fought over the noon dishes! We were supposed to have

speaker: KP
words: taken turns, and I guess we did pretty well on that one. And I

speaker: KP
words: learned to make bread, and must have done pretty well, I've forgotten

speaker: KP
words: how since, for some reason or other. But anyway, I used to make

speaker: KP
words: bread and I liked to iron. My mother hated to iron, and so she said

speaker: KP
words: that worked out just right. She didn't mind the washing, but I re

speaker: KP
words: member I did the washing, too. And that was in the days when we had

speaker: KP
words: a little washing machine that had the thing that you grabbed it and

speaker: KP
words: run it back and forth, like this, like two washboards, they were

speaker: KP
words: rounded, like this and then you rubbed it back and forth, like that.

speaker: KP
words: Never had a wringer, she had to wring out by hand. We set it outside

speaker: KP
words: on the ground and we washed. We had a hydrant out there so we could

speaker: KP
words: have cold water and we had to heat the water in the house. To be

speaker: KP
words: gin with we heated it in a boiler, in fact, I think my mother usually

speaker: KP
words: boiled the white clothes so we always heated a boiler full of water

speaker: KP
words: and that's how we got oujf hot water to wash. Even though our stove

speaker: KP
words: had a reservoir on it. So we had hot water there, too, but we had

speaker: KP
words: to heat extra water when we waited to wash. And we washed and hung

speaker: KP
words: our clothes out on a line. And for some reason or other, seemed like

speaker: KP
words: we had quite a bit of ironing and I remember I used to like to iron.

speaker: KP
words: I don't know where I lost that flavor! (Chuckles) I don't like to

speaker: KP
words: iron now! But anyway, we had to clean our own rooms and clean the

speaker: KP
words: stairsteps.

speaker: KP
